network.overall_strategy: This network targets glycine by combining hydrothermal carbon fixation and reductive amination with surface cyanide chemistry, photochemistry, sugar oxidation chemistry, and final biochemical hydrolysis/transamination. The design avoids weak routes flagged previously by treating HCN as an independent prebiotic feedstock rather than deriving it from formaldehyde, by using canonical Strecker/aminonitrile chemistry, by emphasizing glyoxylate as a central hub reached through multiple better-supported routes, and by connecting hydrothermal products such as formate, acetate, glycolaldehyde, pyruvate, and alanine to surface and biochemical transformations. Ten distinct pathways to glycine are provided, converging mainly through aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, formaldehyde, HCN, and glycine itself.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formate/formic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of CO2 to formate on Fe-S minerals is among the best-supported vent reactions and provides a core hydrothermal feedstock for downstream carbon fixation and surface transfer (He et al., 2010; Barge et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to acetate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal setting with strong redox gradients

reactions.mechanism: Sequential C1 reduction and C-C coupling on Fe-S/Fe0 surfaces produces acetate as an acetyl-thioester analogue endpoint.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from CO2 under hydrothermal reducing conditions is central to iron-sulfur world and acetyl-CoA analog chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: CO2 hydrogenation and partial reduction on metal oxide/sulfide surfaces furnishes C1 carbonyl products including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite-catalyzed CO2/CO hydrogenation and FTT-type chemistry can generate reduced C1 carbonyls that seed sugar chemistry after transfer to surface settings (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal C-C coupling to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal interface, concentrated by pores

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1 species undergo mineral-assisted C-C coupling to yield the C2 aldehyde glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Although more indirect than formate formation, mineral-assisted generation of small aldehydes is plausible within hydrothermal reduction networks and provides a bridge to surface aldol chemistry; included as a cautious connecting step grounded in hydrothermal carbonyl chemistry rather than unsupported nitrile invention (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2
reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-mediated reduction and C-C bond formation within a reductive tricarboxylic-acid-like network yields pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate production in abiotic rTCA-like networks on metal catalysts has experimental support, though lower than for formate/acetate; it is retained because α-keto acids are central hub metabolites in prebiotic protometabolism (Muchowska et al., 2017; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms an imine with ammonia, followed by mineral-assisted reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids on mineral surfaces is a plausible route to amino acids under hydrothermal conditions and provides alanine for later transamination to glycine (Huber & Wachtershauser, 2006; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface formaldehyde oligomerization to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pools with UV exposure and concentration cycles

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically assisted formose initiation dimerizes/stabilizes formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde, with borate suppressing tar formation.
reactions.reasoning: Formose-type entry to glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde is a standard prebiotic sugar route, and borate improves selectivity/stability of small sugars in surface settings (Ricardo et al., 2004; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface aldol extension of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol condensation between glycolaldehyde and formaldehyde forms glyceraldehyde, stabilized by borate complexation.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol growth from glycolaldehyde to triose sugars is a core formose process and provides an alternative route into glyoxylate via later oxidation/fragmentation chemistry without relying on unsupported pyruvate cleavage (Ricardo et al., 2004; Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pools, anoxic but strongly photochemical

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation of the aldehyde/alcohol pair gives the α-keto acid glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a key prebiotic hub in sugar and α-keto acid chemistry, and its formation from small oxygenates in UV-active surface settings is substantially more defensible than the previously criticized pyruvate-fragmentation route (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation/retro-aldol route from glyceraldehyde to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-exposed geothermal field; borate stabilizes triose prior to oxidative fragmentation

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation and carbon-carbon bond cleavage of glyceraldehyde furnishes glyoxylate plus formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This route is included as a moderated, literature-guided oxidation/fragmentation entry rather than a simple unsupported cleavage claim; small sugar oxidation networks can feed α-keto acids such as glyoxylate under UV-active prebiotic conditions (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface cyanide photochemistry to formamide
reactions.mechanism: Hydration and photoactivation of HCN produces formamide as a reactive nitrogenous intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a well-established prebiotic product/intermediate from cyanide-rich chemistry and supplies a more defensible nitrogen branch than the previously rejected cyanogen route (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface cyanide-derived urea formation
reactions.mechanism: Condensation of formamide-derived carbonyl nitrogen chemistry with ammonia gives urea under dehydrating conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Urea is a plausible concentrated surface nitrogen carrier from formamide/ammonia systems and can participate in carbamyl and aminating chemistry relevant to amino acid synthesis (Saladino et al., 2012; Becker et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde and ammonia form an imine/iminium equivalent that is trapped by cyanide to yield aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the strongest canonical prebiotic route to the direct glycine precursor; it avoids the unsupported HCN-from-formaldehyde and cyanogen branches criticized previously (Miller, 1957; Elsila et al., 2007; Danger et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Alternative Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from glycolaldehyde fragmentation feed

conditions.other: UV-assisted carbonyl equilibration in shallow ponds

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde serves as a C2 carbonyl feed that, via surface-assisted retrocarbonyl equilibration/formaldehyde release and imine capture by cyanide, replenishes aminoacetonitrile formation.
reactions.reasoning: Included as a connected alternative entry into the same aminonitrile hub from hydrothermal sugar feedstocks; this is somewhat more speculative than direct formaldehyde Strecker chemistry but remains chemically closer to known carbonyl-cyanide chemistry than the rejected routes from cyanogen or direct HCN reduction. Speculative bridging step acknowledged (Danger et al., 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or prolonged aqueous hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration and hydrolysis passes through amide to carboxylate, yielding glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine is a canonical completion of Strecker chemistry and one of the most robust routes to glycine under prebiotic conditions (Miller, 1957; Danger et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Biochemical reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: anoxic pool or mineral interface with reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate forms an imine with ammonia and is reduced to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Conversion of glyoxylate to glycine by reductive amination is chemically straightforward and metabolically relevant, making glyoxylate a powerful convergence hub (Muchowska et al., 2019; Stubbs et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination of glyoxylate using alanine

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool; amino transfer between alpha-keto acid and amino acid

reactions.mechanism: Alanine donates its amino group to glyoxylate, generating glycine and regenerating pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: This is a strong network-closing transamination step linking hydrothermal alanine/pyruvate chemistry to surface glyoxylate chemistry while avoiding unsupported carbon-shortening of pyruvate to glycine (Weber, 1981; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical urea-assisted conversion of glyoxylate and ammonia to glycine

conditions.other: dehydration-rehydration cycles with concentrated nitrogen carriers

reactions.mechanism: Urea acts as a dehydrating/activating nitrogen carrier that facilitates amination of glyoxylate toward glycine under cycling conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This is a more speculative but chemically bounded route that uses a plausible surface-generated nitrogen carrier to enhance glyoxylate amination, addressing network completeness without repeating unsupported nitrile inventions. Speculative but justifiable as a concentration-assisted amination step (Saladino et al., 2012; Becker et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Direct surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Canonical glycine synthesis from formaldehyde, ammonia, and exogenous HCN via aminoacetonitrile followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction generates formaldehyde, which is transported to the surface for Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde-assisted aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde seeds glycolaldehyde chemistry, feeding an alternative entry into aminoacetonitrile before hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C2 aldehydes are oxidized at the surface to glyoxylate, then reductively aminated in biochemical pools to glycine.

pathways.name: Surface formose-glyoxylate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde undergoes surface formose initiation to glycolaldehyde, oxidation to glyoxylate, then reductive amination to glycine.

pathways.name: Triose oxidation route to glycine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde build glyceraldehyde at the surface, which oxidatively fragments to glyoxylate and is then aminated.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate-alanine transamination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is converted to alanine, which later transaminates surface-derived glyoxylate to yield glycine and regenerate pyruvate.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-alanine route coupled to formose glyoxylate branch
pathways.description: Alanine from hydrothermal pyruvate meets glyoxylate generated from hydrothermal formaldehyde through surface formose chemistry, then transaminates to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN-formamide-urea assisted glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Surface HCN hydration gives formamide and then urea, which participates in glyoxylate amination to glycine after hydrothermal-to-surface carbon transfer.

pathways.name: Acetate/formate-fed hydrothermal carbonyl route to glycine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduction produces formate and acetate that feed carbonyl chemistry toward formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde, then onward through glyoxylate to glycine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongest pathway in the network. Formaldehyde + ammonia + HCN to aminoacetonitrile is a canonical prebiotic Strecker-type route, and hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine is chemically plausible under aqueous prebiotic conditions. This route is short, coherent, and directly achieves the target with minimal speculative chemistry.
suggestions: Specify hydrolysis conditions for aminoacetonitrile more explicitly, such as prolonged aqueous heating or mineral-assisted hydrolysis.
Clarify whether formaldehyde is supplied exogenously or inherited from hydrothermal synthesis in this pathway definition.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good environmental linkage from hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface cyanide chemistry, but the key weakness is the proposed direct hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of formaldehyde from CO2 and H2. That step is substantially less established than formate or acetate production and lowers confidence in the otherwise strong Strecker branch.
suggestions: Replace direct hydrothermal formaldehyde formation with a better-supported route through methanol/formate-derived surface photochemistry or atmospheric input.
Add explicit mineral agents and conditions for formaldehyde generation to improve plausibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is weakened by two issues: uncertain hydrothermal formaldehyde synthesis and a poorly justified alternative Strecker step from glycolaldehyde directly to aminoacetonitrile. Glycolaldehyde would more naturally lead to serine-like products or higher aminonitriles rather than glycine precursor aminoacetonitrile without fragmentation steps that are not represented.
suggestions: Revise the glycolaldehyde branch to produce a chemically appropriate aminonitrile product, or insert a fragmentation step to formaldehyde before Strecker chemistry.
Avoid using glycolaldehyde as a direct precursor to aminoacetonitrile unless mechanistic support is provided.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The terminal glyoxylate reductive amination step is plausible and metabolically relevant, but the upstream formation of glycolaldehyde from formate and formaldehyde in hydrothermal settings is weakly supported, and oxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate under explicitly anoxic prebiotic surface conditions needs better justification. The route is conceptually coherent but chemically uneven.
suggestions: Provide a stronger oxidation mechanism for glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate under anoxic conditions, such as photoredox or mineral-mediated pathways.
Use a more established route to glycolaldehyde, for example formose chemistry from formaldehyde on borate-containing surfaces.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a reasonable surface-to-biochemical pathway in outline: formose chemistry can generate glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, and glyoxylate can feed glycine formation. However, the conversion of glyceraldehyde to glyoxylate plus formaldehyde is too compressed and mechanistically underspecified, making the convergence into glyoxylate less convincing.
suggestions: Split the glyceraldehyde-to-glyoxylate transformation into separate oxidation and cleavage steps.
Specify the oxidant or photocatalytic mineral system enabling this conversion under anoxic conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As with P5, the triose branch is attractive conceptually but depends on an insufficiently justified glyceraldehyde-to-glyoxylate transformation. The later glyoxylate amination step is plausible, but the route needs better mechanistic granularity to be compelling.
suggestions: Add explicit intermediate(s) such as glyceric acid or tartronate if oxidation chemistry is intended.
Support the triose oxidation branch with a more realistic mineral-photochemical sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong metabolism-like route. Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis is plausible though still challenging, reductive amination to alanine is reasonable on mineral surfaces, and transamination of glyoxylate with alanine to yield glycine and pyruvate is especially coherent because it closes a keto acid/amino acid exchange cycle. This is one of the best-integrated pathways in the network, assuming glyoxylate is available from another branch.
suggestions: Clarify the source of glyoxylate within the explicit sequence if this pathway is intended to stand alone.
Highlight mineral or cofactor analogs that could facilitate prebiotic transamination.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is an effective cross-environment pathway: hydrothermal generation of pyruvate and alanine is coupled to a surface route to glyoxylate, then closed by transamination to glycine. The main limitation is the moderate uncertainty in glycolaldehyde oxidation to glyoxylate, but the pathway architecture is strong and demonstrates environmental interconnection well.
suggestions: Strengthen the glyoxylate-forming step with more explicit mineral photoredox conditions.
Consider replacing direct oxidation of glycolaldehyde with a two-step sequence through glycolate/glyoxal if better supported.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest pathway. HCN to formamide under UV is plausible in broad terms, but the formamide-plus-ammonia to urea step is not a standard prebiotic route in this context, and the final urea-assisted conversion of glyoxylate and ammonia to glycine is highly speculative and not represented as a clear established mechanism. The pathway does not convincingly deliver glycine.
suggestions: Remove the urea-assisted glycine formation step unless direct experimental support is available.
Use formamide chemistry to access HCN-derived intermediates or nucleobases rather than forcing a glycine route through urea.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hydrothermal front end is partly strong because formate and acetate production from CO2/H2 are plausible, but the claimed carbonyl coupling route toward glycolaldehyde remains the weak link. Once glyoxylate is available, conversion to glycine is credible. Overall this is a partially plausible but chemically uneven route.
suggestions: Do not rely on acetate/formate coupling to glycolaldehyde without stronger support.
Route formate into more defensible one-carbon chemistry or treat acetate as a separate feedstock rather than a direct precursor to glycine.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it has sufficient size, clear hub molecules, multiple convergence points, and good cross-environment connectivity from hydrothermal to surface to biochemical chemistry. The strongest parts are the Strecker/aminoacetonitrile route to glycine, the pyruvate-to-alanine branch, and the glyoxylate transamination/reductive amination endpoints. The weakest parts are several upstream transformations that are chemically underjustified, especially direct hydrothermal formaldehyde synthesis, hydrothermal formate/formaldehyde coupling to glycolaldehyde, the use of glycolaldehyde as a direct precursor to aminoacetonitrile, and the urea-assisted glycine route. Environmental interconnection is good in concept, but some pathway labels appear to overstate support. Overall, the network is acceptable as a broad exploratory synthesis map, but not all ten pathways are equally viable and the average confidence is pulled below approval threshold by several speculative reactions. A reduced network centered on P1, P7, and P8 would be substantially stronger.
